title: Random Forests: Trees with Random Choices
concept: A Random Forest is an extension of bagging that builds diverse decision trees by restricting each split to a random subset of features, preventing any single strong feature from dominating.

options.text: Training multiple models in parallel on bootstrap samples, then aggregating them.
Pruning a single decision tree recursively.
Measuring distance to the nearest centroid.

exercises.explain: Correct! Bagging combines bootstrap sampling with aggregation.

cards.title: The copycat problem
cards.body: If you train 100 decision trees on slightly different bootstrap samples, but your dataset has one extremely strong feature (e.g. 'Income' for predicting wealth), *every single tree* will choose to split on Income at the very top. Your trees will look almost identical, ruining the diversity of your crowd. We need a way to force the trees to be different.

cards.title: Random Forest: Feature randomness
cards.body: A Random Forest solves the copycat problem. When building each tree, it uses bagging to select rows, but it also restricts each node split to a **random subset of features** (columns). If Income is blocked from a tree's root, it's forced to look at other features, discovering hidden patterns.

cards.title: Detectives with restricted clues
cards.body: If 10 detectives investigate a case, and the murder weapon is visible, they will all focus on it. If you lock the weapon in a drawer for some detectives, they are forced to study the footprints, the timetable, or the motives. By restricting their clues, you get a much broader, richer investigation.

cards.title: Feature selection at the node
cards.body: A dataset has features: Age, Income, Location, Gender. At the root of Tree 1, the algorithm randomly selects only `[Location, Gender]` as candidates. It splits on Location. At Tree 2's root, it selects `[Age, Income]` and splits on Income. The trees develop completely different questions, maximizing ensemble diversity.

cards.title: Random Forests don't need a test set to monitor error
cards.body: You might think you always need a validation split to check a forest's health. Because of bagging, about 37% of training rows are left out of each bootstrap sample (called **Out-Of-Bag** or OOB data). We can test each tree on its OOB rows, giving us a built-in error estimate without peeking at the test set.

cards.title: Bagging vs. Random Forest
cards.body: How feature selection differs in tree ensembles:

left.text:
Random rows (bootstrap).
Looks at ALL features at each split.
Strong features dominate all trees.

right.text:
Random rows + Random features.
Restricts features at each split.
Guarantees highly diverse trees.

exercises.prompt: How does a Random Forest introduce diversity among its decision trees?

options.text: By training each tree on random rows (bagging) and limiting each split to a random subset of features.
By forcing all trees to have the exact same split questions.
By scaling all feature numbers between 0 and 1.
By deleting 50% of the trees after training.

exercises.explain: Correct! Random Forests use both row bootstrapping and feature subset selection to maximize tree diversity.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Out-Of-Bag (OOB) data refers to the training rows that were left out of a specific tree's bootstrap sample.
exercises.explain: True! About 37% of data is left out of each bootstrap sample, serving as a built-in validation set.

exercises.sentence: By restricting splits to a random subset of features, Random Forests prevent a single ___ feature from dominating all trees.
exercises.explain: Restricting features allows weaker but useful features to get selected, improving diversity.

exercises.prompt: Which process describes a Random Forest building a split?

options.text: A node randomly selects 3 out of 10 available features, then finds the best split among those 3.
A node evaluates all 10 features and splits on the absolute best one.
A node splits the dataset in half alphabetically.

exercises.explain: Correct! Limiting the candidates to a random subset before splitting is the hallmark of a Random Forest.

exercises.prompt: A dataset predicting customer churn has one highly predictive column, 'Last Login Date'. In a standard bagging ensemble of 50 trees, almost every tree splits on this column first. In a Random Forest of 50 trees, only some split on it first. What is the impact?

options.text: The Random Forest is more diverse and robust, because trees without the login feature are forced to find patterns in other columns (like payment history or app usage).
The Random Forest is less accurate, because 'Last Login Date' should be in every tree.
The Random Forest runs slower because it has to calculate entropy for more columns.
The trees are identical in both ensembles.

exercises.explain: Exactly! By blocking the dominant feature from some trees, we force them to learn from other columns, discovering secondary patterns. This creates a smarter, more robust crowd. Next, let's look at why this algorithm dominates tabular ML!
